data.title: Intel disputes $421M fine from EU regulators stemming from 16-year-old case: report
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) attorneys disputed the magnitude of a $421.4M fine levied against the semiconductor company by the European Commission for allegedly blocking rival firms from the market, according to Reuters.

The case dates back to 2009, when the Commission hit Intel with a $1.18B fine for preventing fair competition by Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), according to the report. Intel appealed the case to the General Court, and successfully reduced the scope of the fine in 2022. However, judges still found Intel culpable of making payments to HP (HPE), Acer and Lenovo to stall or block them from using products made by competitors during a period ranging from 2002 to 2006.

"The Commission cannot sustain a finding that there was an overall strategy to foreclose competitors from the entire x86 chips market," Intel lawyer Daniel Beard said to judges on Friday, according to the report. 

"The naked restrictions can't be treated as in effect of equal weight to each of the pricing practices which were overturned," Beard added. "Nor do they have the same sort of cumulative effect or strategic weight."

A ruling on the final size of the fine is expected sometime in the next few months, the report said.

data.title: FTX to pay $5B to creditors as part of defunct crypto exchange's recovery plan
data.description:
FTX (FTT-USD), the now-defunct crypto exchange founded by convicted felon Sam Bankman-Fried, is returning more than $5B to creditors starting May 30, as part of its broader recovery plan to manage assets for creditor repayments.

Holders of allowed claims in the reorganization plan's convenience and non-convenience classes that have completed the pre-distribution requirements should expect to receive funds from their selected distribution service provider -- either custodians Bitgo or Kraken -- within one to three from May 30, the FTX estate said.

The company's second distribution has been structured as: allowed Class 5A dotcom customer entitlement claims will receive a 72% distribution; allowed Class 5B U.S. customer entitlement claims will receive a 54% distribution; allowed Classes 6A general unsecured claims and 6B digital asset loan claims will each receive a 61% distribution; and allowed Class 7 convenience claims will receive a 120% distribution.

"The scope and magnitude of the FTX creditor base makes this an unprecedented distribution process, and today's announcement reflects the outstanding success of the recovery and coordination efforts of our team of professionals," FTX CEO John J. Ray III said in a statement. "Our focus remains on recovering more for creditors and resolving outstanding claims."

Last August, U.S. District Judge Peter Castel ordered the defunct exchange and its trading partner, Alameda Research, to pay $12.7B to their creditors, a move that ended a 20-month lawsuit filed by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission.

data.title: Upexi reports Q1 results
data.description:

data.title: Protectionist policies are far more damaging than trade deficits themselves – analyst
data.description:
IndyCar driver Pato O’Ward’s recent live television frustration over unexpected customs bills for his race helmets has become a striking example of how new U.S. tariffs affect everyday Americans, according to Elisabeth Dellinger, a manager in Fisher Investments’ Portfolio Management Communications Group and Senior Editor of MarketMinder for Fisher Investments.

In a recent article, she noted that this incident reveals a deeper economic misconception among U.S.politicians who view trade deficits as inherently problematic.

“Each seems to want what the other has,” she wrote, referring to how nations with trade surpluses often envy deficit countries and vice versa.

China’s trade surplus, approaching $1T in 2024, presents a paradoxical economic case study that challenges conventional political rhetoric, according to Dellinger. While many U.S. politicians would view such a massive surplus as evidence of economic strength, she explained that from China’s perspective, it actually represents troubling industrial overcapacity and dangerous dependence on foreign markets.

Trade surpluses often indicate an economy producing more than it consumes, which can stem from misaligned investment priorities, she went on. This imbalance frequently results from policies aimed at boosting exports rather than developing domestic consumption, while artificially weakened currencies intended to increase exports ultimately make life more expensive for citizens.

These large trade surpluses necessitate capital controls because countries cannot simultaneously maintain managed currencies and free capital movement, Dellinger said, and added that this stems from a fundamental accounting identity: trade surpluses correspond to financial account deficits, meaning export-heavy economies see more money flowing out through financial channels than coming in.

Market performance tells a revealing story about the true impact of trade balances, she said. While the U.S. stock market (SP500), (DJI), (COMP:IND) has risen significantly since 2007 despite growing trade deficits, China’s market (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:GXC) has remained relatively flat despite its soaring surplus.

“Markets are screaming it from the rafters!” she said, suggesting that investors view protectionist policies like tariffs as far more damaging than trade deficits themselves.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow are muted as softer inflation data keep sentiments high
data.description:
Wall Street major averages are muted on Friday as the S&P 500 hoped to extend its four-day straight rally, while investors recieved the latest consumer sentiment report.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.1%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.1%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.1%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 2 basis points to 4.40%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 2 basis points to 3.93%.

Wall Street on Thursday ended mixed, but its benchmark S&P 500 index extended a rally to four straight days and further solidified itself in positive territory for the year.

“Markets continued to advance over the last 24 hours, as weak U.S. inflation data and lower oil prices raised expectations that the Federal Reserve would still cut rates this year,” Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

There were several factors driving the rally, but a critically important one was the U.S. PPI data for April. It showed inflation pressures were much softer than expected, Allen added.

The April producer price index (PPI) report showed both headline and core producer prices unexpectedly declining on a M/M basis.

U.S. import and export prices surprised to the upside in April. 

Housing starts rose in April; building permits dropped more than expected.

On the economic side, the University of Michigan's Consumer Sentiment Index edged down to 50.8 in May (preliminary) versus the 53.1 consensus level.

data.title: Bets against utility stocks increase in April; AES most shorted
data.description:
Bets against Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) rose in April compared to the end of March, with AES Corp (AES) being the most shorted stock in the sector and Sempra (SRE) taking the spot for the least shorted.

Average short interest across S&P 500 utilities stocks was 2.36% of shares float at the end of April, slightly up from 2.29% at the end of March.

The S&P 500’s Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) was up 7.40% so far this year, outperforming the broader S&P500 market, which gained 0.60% during the same period.

Stocks with the least and largest short positions

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

AES Corp (AES) had 40.69 million shares sold short as of April 30, or 5.74% of shares float.

Evergy (EVRG) was the second-most shorted stock with short interest of 4.60% followed by CenterPoint Energy (CNP) with 4.51%, WEC Energy Group (WEC) with 4.19% and American Electric Power (AEP) with 3.87%.

Sempra (SRE) was the least shorted stock, with 7.45 million shares sold short or 1.14% of the shares float, followed by Duke Energy (DUK) and Ameren (AEE) with short interest of 1.33% and 1.45% respectively.

Industry analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

Independent Power & Renewable Electricity Producers was the most shorted industry within the sector with 4.50% short interest as of April end, compared to 3.45% a month ago.

Gas Utilities stood at the second spot with 3.33% short interest as of the end of April, which increased from 3.08% at the end of March.

Multi utilities stocks took the third spot with 2.49% short interest as of April end, compared to 2.32% at the end of March.

Bets against the Electric Utilities sector as a whole have decreased to 2.11% at the end of April, compared to 2.17% a month ago.

data.title: Qualcomm partners with UAE telecom company to drive edge AI, 5G technologies
data.description:
Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) has partnered with e&, a United Arab Emirates telecom company, to develop advanced connectivity, 5G and edge AI technologies across government, enterprise and industrial sectors in the UAE and beyond, the companies announced today.

The collaboration is expected to benefit the UAE economy, as Qualcomm will utilize its new engineering center located in the capital city of Abu Dhabi.

Qualcomm plans to help develop edge AI gateways and enhance its devices powered by its Snapdragon platform. These will integrate large language models, which support on-device generative AI capabilities and inferencing, the companies said.

"We look forward to working with e& to accelerate innovation and technology advancement across its ecosystem of enterprise and government customers in the UAE and beyond," said Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon.

"e& and Qualcomm Technologies have a history of cooperation, and this new agreement will help drive the digital transformation of enterprises significantly enhancing the UAE's role in the global technology landscape," said e& CEO Hatem Dowidar.

The UAE has been busy this week landing deals with the U.S. and U.S. tech companies. On Thursday, U.S. President Donald Trump and UAE President Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, unveiled the first phase of a new 5 gigawatt artificial intelligence campus in Abu Dhabi. It was also reported that the U.S. has a preliminary agreement with the United Arab Emirates to allow it to import 500,000 of Nvidia's (NVDA) advanced AI chips per year, beginning this year.

Earlier this week, it was reported that OpenAI is considering opening a new data center in the Middle Eastern nation.

data.title: Acadia Pharmaceuticals jumps after win in patent suit over Nuplazid
data.description:
Acadia Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:ACAD) soared 20% after a court ruling in patent suit regarding its drug Nuplazid, which is used to treat Parkinson's disease. 

The court ruled that Aurobindo Pharma infringes claims 4 and 5 of its '721 patent,  U.S. District Court Judge Gregory B. Williams wrote in a ruling on Friday. 

"The court concludes that Defendants failed to demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that claims 4 and 5 of US Patent No. 11,452,721 are invalid," Williams wrote. 

The latest victory comes after Acadia (NASDAQ:ACAD) surged 35% on Dec. 13, 2023 after it won summary judgement in patent suit over Nuplazid.

data.title: CoreWeave jumps as investors pile in after Q1 results
data.description:
• CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) shares rose more than 16% on Friday, as investors appeared to reassess the cloud service provider's first-quarter results that received high praise on Wall Street.
  • Several sell-side firms, including Wells Fargo, Morgan Stanley and Bank of America, raised their price targets on CoreWeave, with one analyst stating it has become a “core holding” for investors.
  • The company also disclosed in its 10-Q filing that OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT, will pay it up to $4B through April 2029 to provide cloud computing capacity. 
  • For the period ending March 31, CoreWeave said revenue rose 420.3% year-over-year to $981.6M. The company ended the period with a revenue backlog of $25.9B, including remaining performance obligations of $14.7B.
  • CoreWeave went public in March, raising $1.5B in its offering.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: SPCE, AMAT, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged higher while Treasury yields slipped on Friday as the market's focus turned to today's options expirations.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Virgin Galactic (NYSE:SPCE) +15% — Shares climbed after the space tourism company posted better-than-expected Q1 results and reaffirmed its timeline for upcoming spaceflights. The first mission with its new Spaceship carrying research payloads remains on track for summer 2026, with private astronaut flights scheduled for fall 2026. CEO Michael Colglazier emphasized the long-term value of assets being developed during the company’s pre-revenue phase, projecting a scalable and profitable business model supported by cost efficiencies and a premium customer experience.
  • TSS (NASDAQ:TSSI) +56% — Shares soared after the firm posted Q1 results with revenue surging 523% Y/Y and diluted EPS rising to $0.12 from just above breakeven. Adjusted EBITDA increased more than tenfold from the prior year. CEO Darryll Dewan expressed optimism for 2025 and beyond, citing the launch of production at a new facility as a key growth driver. He reaffirmed expectations for H1 2025 revenue to exceed H2 2024 levels and projected FY2025 adjusted EBITDA to be at least 50% higher than 2024. The CEO also highlighted the new facility’s significant power capacity as a differentiator in the evolving AI landscape.
  • Charter (NASDAQ:CHTR) +4% — Shares rose after the company announced it will combine with Cox Communications in a cash-and-stock deal, valuing the rival cable operator at an enterprise value of $34.5B. The transaction includes $21.9B in equity and $12.6B in net debt and obligations. Charter will acquire Cox’s commercial fiber, managed IT, and cloud businesses, while Cox Enterprises will contribute its residential cable business to Charter Holdings. Following the deal, Cox Enterprises will hold about 23% of the combined entity’s fully diluted shares. The merger is expected to close alongside the previously announced Liberty Broadband merger, after which Liberty Broadband will no longer be a direct shareholder in Charter.
  • Archer Aviation (NYSE:ACHR) +13% — Shares surged after the company was named the Official Air Taxi Provider of the 2028 Los Angeles Olympic and Paralympic Games. Archer’s Midnight eVTOL aircraft will support the Games by transporting VIPs, fans, and stakeholders and by electrifying vertiport hubs at key venues. The partnership includes integration with emergency services and support for Team USA. Archer plans to deploy its piloted electric air taxis across a network of vertiports in the LA area, including LAX, Hollywood, Santa Monica, and major stadiums, offering 10–20 minute flights during the event expected to host over 15M visitors.

Biggest stock losers

  • Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) -6% — Shares fell after the semiconductor equipment maker posted mixed FQ2 results, with revenue missing estimates despite 7% Y/Y growth. For FQ3, the company guided revenue between $6.7B and $7.7B, with the $7.2B midpoint roughly in line with the $7.19B consensus. It forecasts an adjusted gross margin of 48.3% and EPS between $2.15 and $2.55, with the midpoint of $2.35 slightly above the $2.31 consensus.
  • Take-Two Interactive (NASDAQ:TTWO) -3% — Shares fell after the company posted mixed FQ4 results and issued a disappointing FY2026 outlook. Management guided for a full-year loss of $2.45 to $2.79 per share on revenue of $5.95B to $6.05B, far below analyst expectations of $6.89 EPS and $7.81B in revenue. Net bookings are projected between $5.9B and $6B. For FQ1, the company expects a per-share loss of $0.65 to $0.78, vs. consensus for a profit of $0.36, while revenue guidance of $1.35B to $1.4B topped expectations of $1.28B.
  • Doximity (NYSE:DOCS) -14% — Shares fell despite the company posting upbeat FQ4 results with 17% Y/Y top-line growth, as a weaker-than-expected outlook weighed on sentiment. For Q1, Doximity guided for revenue between $139M and $140M, below the consensus estimate of $143.4M. For FY2026, it expects revenue between $619M and $631M, also shy of the $639.4M Street view.
  • Globant (NYSE:GLOB) -29% — Shares plummeted after the company missed both revenue and earnings estimates for Q1 and was downgraded to Neutral by Piper Sandler. The firm reported revenue of $611.1M and adjusted EPS of $1.50. Piper Sandler, which had already lowered estimates last month, cited persistent macro headwinds, particularly in LATAM and tariff-exposed industries. While analysts see long-term potential in Globant’s offerings, they expressed concerns over near-term demand and the company's limited ability to outperform broader market trends.
